question: Let $F_0, F_1, \dots$ be the sequence of Fibonacci numbers, with $F_0 = 0$, $F_1 = 1$, and $F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2}$ for $n \geq 2$. For $m > 2$, let $R_m$ be the remainder when the product $\prod_{k=1}^{F_m-1} k^k$ is divided by $F_m$. Prove that $R_m$ is also a Fibonacci number.
solution:
We can check directly that $R_3=R_4=1$ are Virahanka--Fibonacci numbers; henceforth we will assume $m \geq 5$.

Denote the product $\prod_{k=1}^{F_m-1} k^k$ by $A$. Note that if $F_m$ is composite, say $F_m = ab$ for $a,b>1$ integers, then $A$ is divisible by $a^a b^b$ and thus by $F_m=ab$; it follows that $R_m=0=F_0$ when $F_m$ is composite.

Now suppose that $F_m$ is prime. Since $F_{2n} = F_n(F_{n+1}+F_{n-1})$ for all $n$, $F_m$ is composite if $m>4$ is even; thus we must have that $m$ is odd. Write $p=F_m$, and use $\equiv$ to denote congruence $\pmod p$. Then we have
\[
A = \prod_{k=1}^{p-1} (p-k)^{p-k} \equiv \prod_{k=1}^{p-1} (-k)^{p-k} = (-1)^{p(p-1)/2} \prod_{k=1}^{p-1} k^{p-k}
\]
and consequently
\begin{align*}
A^2 &\equiv (-1)^{p(p-1)/2} \prod_{k=1}^{p-1} (k^k k^{p-k}) \\
&= (-1)^{p(p-1)/2}((p-1)!)^p \\
&\equiv (-1)^{p(p+1)/2},
\end{align*}
where the final congruence follows from Wilson's Theorem. Now observe that when $m$ is odd, $p=F_m$ must be congruent to either $1$ or $2 \pmod{4}$: this follows from inspection of the Virahanka--Fibonacci sequence mod $4$, which has period $6$: $1,1,2,3,1,0,1,1,\ldots$. It follows that $A^2 \equiv (-1)^{p(p+1)/2} = -1$.

On the other hand, by the Kepler--Cassini identity
\[
F_n^2 = (-1)^{n+1} + F_{n-1}F_{n+1}
\]
with $n=m-1$, we have $F_{m-1}^2 \equiv (-1)^m = -1$. Thus we have
$0 \equiv A^2 - F_{m-1}^2 \equiv (A-F_{m-1})(A-F_{m-2})$. Since $p$ is prime, it must be the case that either $A=F_{m-1}$ or $A=F_{m-2}$, and we are done.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
The Kepler--Cassini identity first appears in a letter of Kepler from 1608.
Noam Elkies has scanned the \href{https://people.math.harvard.edu/~elkies/Kepler_XVI_p157.jpg}{relevant page of Kepler's collected works} (slightly NSFW if your boss can read Latin).

descriptive_long.question: Let $fibzero, fibone, \dots$ be the sequence of Fibonacci numbers, with $fibzero = 0$, $fibone = 1$, and $fibgeneral = fibprev + F_{indexvar-2}$ for $indexvar \geq 2$. For $fibstep > 2$, let $fibremainder$ be the remainder when the product $\prod_{loopvar=1}^{fibbefore} loopvar^{loopvar}$ is divided by $fibcurrent$. Prove that $fibremainder$ is also a Fibonacci number.
descriptive_long.solution:
We can check directly that $R_3=R_4=1$ are Virahanka--Fibonacci numbers; henceforth we will assume $fibstep \geq 5$.

Denote the product $\prod_{loopvar=1}^{fibbefore} loopvar^{loopvar}$ by $bigproduct$. Note that if $fibcurrent$ is composite, say $fibcurrent = divisorone divisortwo$ for $divisorone,divisortwo>1$ integers, then $bigproduct$ is divisible by $divisorone^{divisorone} divisortwo^{divisortwo}$ and thus by $fibcurrent=divisorone divisortwo$; it follows that $fibremainder=0=fibzero$ when $fibcurrent$ is composite.

Now suppose that $fibcurrent$ is prime. Since $fibdouble = fibgeneral(fibnext+fibprev)$ for all $indexvar$, $fibcurrent$ is composite if $fibstep>4$ is even; thus we must have that $fibstep$ is odd. Write $primefib=fibcurrent$, and use $\equiv$ to denote congruence $\pmod{primefib}$. Then we have
\[
bigproduct = \prod_{loopvar=1}^{primefib-1} (primefib-loopvar)^{primefib-loopvar} \equiv \prod_{loopvar=1}^{primefib-1} (-loopvar)^{primefib-loopvar} = (-1)^{primefib(primefib-1)/2} \prod_{loopvar=1}^{primefib-1} loopvar^{primefib-loopvar}
\]
and consequently
\begin{align*}
bigproduct^2 &\equiv (-1)^{primefib(primefib-1)/2} \prod_{loopvar=1}^{primefib-1} (loopvar^{loopvar} loopvar^{primefib-loopvar}) \\
&= (-1)^{primefib(primefib-1)/2}((primefib-1)!)^{primefib} \\
&\equiv (-1)^{primefib(primefib+1)/2},
\end{align*}
where the final congruence follows from Wilson's Theorem. Now observe that when $fibstep$ is odd, $primefib=fibcurrent$ must be congruent to either $1$ or $2 \pmod{4}$: this follows from inspection of the Virahanka--Fibonacci sequence mod $4$, which has period $6$: $1,1,2,3,1,0,1,1,\ldots$. It follows that $bigproduct^2 \equiv (-1)^{primefib(primefib+1)/2} = -1$.

On the other hand, by the Kepler--Cassini identity
\[
fibgeneral^2 = (-1)^{indexvar+1} + fibprev fibnext
\]
with $indexvar=fibstep-1$, we have $fibbefore^2 \equiv (-1)^{fibstep} = -1$. Thus we have
$0 \equiv bigproduct^2 - fibbefore^2 \equiv (bigproduct-fibbefore)(bigproduct-fibtwoback)$. Since $primefib$ is prime, it must be the case that either $bigproduct=fibbefore$ or $bigproduct=fibtwoback$, and we are done.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
The Kepler--Cassini identity first appears in a letter of Kepler from 1608.
Noam Elkies has scanned the \href{https://people.math.harvard.edu/~elkies/Kepler_XVI_p157.jpg}{relevant page of Kepler's collected works} (slightly NSFW if your boss can read Latin).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: Let $compassrose, hoodwink, \dots$ be the sequence of Fibonacci numbers, with $compassrose = 0$, $hoodwink = 1$, and $drumstick = F_{parchment-1} + F_{parchment-2}$ for $parchment \geq 2$. For $labyrinth > 2$, let $silkworm$ be the remainder when the product $\prod_{blackbird=1}^{raincloud} blackbird^{blackbird}$ is divided by $quesadilla$. Prove that $silkworm$ is also a Fibonacci number.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We can check directly that $R_3=R_4=1$ are Virahanka--Fibonacci numbers; henceforth we will assume $labyrinth \geq 5$.

Denote the product $\prod_{blackbird=1}^{raincloud} blackbird^{blackbird}$ by $nightshade$. Note that if $quesadilla$ is composite, say $quesadilla = watershed marigold$ for $watershed,marigold>1$ integers, then $nightshade$ is divisible by $watershed^{watershed} marigold^{marigold}$ and thus by $quesadilla=watershed marigold$; it follows that $silkworm=0=compassrose$ when $quesadilla$ is composite.

Now suppose that $quesadilla$ is prime. Since $F_{2parchment} = F_{parchment}(F_{parchment+1}+F_{parchment-1})$ for all $parchment$, $quesadilla$ is composite if $labyrinth>4$ is even; thus we must have that $labyrinth$ is odd. Write $gemstone=quesadilla$, and use $\equiv$ to denote congruence $\pmod{gemstone}$. Then we have
\[
nightshade = \prod_{blackbird=1}^{gemstone-1} (gemstone-blackbird)^{gemstone-blackbird} \equiv \prod_{blackbird=1}^{gemstone-1} (-blackbird)^{gemstone-blackbird} = (-1)^{gemstone(gemstone-1)/2} \prod_{blackbird=1}^{gemstone-1} blackbird^{gemstone-blackbird}
\]
and consequently
\begin{align*}
nightshade^2 &\equiv (-1)^{gemstone(gemstone-1)/2} \prod_{blackbird=1}^{gemstone-1} (blackbird^{blackbird} blackbird^{gemstone-blackbird}) \\
&= (-1)^{gemstone(gemstone-1)/2}((gemstone-1)!)^{gemstone} \\
&\equiv (-1)^{gemstone(gemstone+1)/2},
\end{align*}
where the final congruence follows from Wilson's Theorem. Now observe that when $labyrinth$ is odd, $gemstone=quesadilla$ must be congruent to either $1$ or $2 \pmod{4}$: this follows from inspection of the Virahanka--Fibonacci sequence mod $4$, which has period $6$: $1,1,2,3,1,0,1,1,\ldots$. It follows that $nightshade^2 \equiv (-1)^{gemstone(gemstone+1)/2} = -1$.

On the other hand, by the Kepler--Cassini identity
\[
F_{parchment}^2 = (-1)^{parchment+1} + F_{parchment-1}F_{parchment+1}
\]
with $parchment=labyrinth-1$, we have $F_{labyrinth-1}^2 \equiv (-1)^{labyrinth} = -1$. Thus we have
$0 \equiv nightshade^2 - F_{labyrinth-1}^2 \equiv (nightshade-F_{labyrinth-1})(nightshade-F_{labyrinth-2})$. Since $gemstone$ is prime, it must be the case that either $nightshade=F_{labyrinth-1}$ or $nightshade=F_{labyrinth-2}$, and we are done.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
The Kepler--Cassini identity first appears in a letter of Kepler from 1608.
Noam Elkies has scanned the \href{https://people.math.harvard.edu/~elkies/Kepler_XVI_p157.jpg}{relevant page of Kepler's collected works} (slightly NSFW if your boss can read Latin).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Let $terminalvalue, finalvalue, \dots$ be the sequence of Fibonacci numbers, with $terminalvalue = 0$, $finalvalue = 1$, and $staticconstant = nextentry + F_{endpoint-2}$ for $endpoint \geq 2$. For $startpoint > 2$, let $completevalue$ be the remainder when the product $\prod_{haltingvar=1}^{nonfibonacci-1} haltingvar^{haltingvar}$ is divided by $nonfibonacci$. Prove that $completevalue$ is also a Fibonacci number.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We can check directly that $R_3=R_4=1$ are Virahanka--Fibonacci numbers; henceforth we will assume $startpoint \geq 5$.

Denote the product $\prod_{haltingvar=1}^{nonfibonacci-1} haltingvar^{haltingvar}$ by $sumtotal$. Note that if $nonfibonacci$ is composite, say $nonfibonacci = multiple\, quotient$ for $multiple, quotient>1$ integers, then $sumtotal$ is divisible by $multiple^{multiple} \, quotient^{quotient}$ and thus by $nonfibonacci=multiple\, quotient$; it follows that $completevalue=0=terminalvalue$ when $nonfibonacci$ is composite.

Now suppose that $nonfibonacci$ is prime. Since $oddindexterm = staticconstant(previousentry+nextentry)$ for all $endpoint$, $nonfibonacci$ is composite if $startpoint>4$ is even; thus we must have that $startpoint$ is odd. Write $composite=nonfibonacci$, and use $\equiv$ to denote congruence $\pmod composite$. Then we have
\[
sumtotal = \prod_{haltingvar=1}^{composite-1} (composite-haltingvar)^{composite-haltingvar} \equiv \prod_{haltingvar=1}^{composite-1} (-haltingvar)^{composite-haltingvar} = (-1)^{composite(composite-1)/2} \prod_{haltingvar=1}^{composite-1} haltingvar^{composite-haltingvar}
\]
and consequently
\begin{align*}
sumtotal^2 &\equiv (-1)^{composite(composite-1)/2} \prod_{haltingvar=1}^{composite-1} (haltingvar^{haltingvar} \, haltingvar^{composite-haltingvar}) \
&= (-1)^{composite(composite-1)/2}((composite-1)!)^{composite} \\
&\equiv (-1)^{composite(composite+1)/2},
\end{align*}
where the final congruence follows from Wilson's Theorem. Now observe that when $startpoint$ is odd, $composite=nonfibonacci$ must be congruent to either $1$ or $2 \pmod{4}$: this follows from inspection of the Virahanka--Fibonacci sequence mod $4$, which has period $6$: $1,1,2,3,1,0,1,1,\ldots$. It follows that $sumtotal^2 \equiv (-1)^{composite(composite+1)/2} = -1$.

On the other hand, by the Kepler--Cassini identity
\[
staticconstant^2 = (-1)^{endpoint+1} + nextentry \, previousentry
\]
with $endpoint=startpoint-1$, we have $forwardentry^2 \equiv (-1)^{startpoint} = -1$. Thus we have
\[
0 \equiv sumtotal^2 - forwardentry^2 \equiv (sumtotal-forwardentry)(sumtotal-furtherentry).
\]
Since $composite$ is prime, it must be the case that either $sumtotal=forwardentry$ or $sumtotal=furtherentry$, and we are done.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
The Kepler--Cassini identity first appears in a letter of Kepler from 1608.
Noam Elkies has scanned the \href{https://people.math.harvard.edu/~elkies/Kepler_XVI_p157.jpg}{relevant page of Kepler's collected works} (slightly NSFW if your boss can read Latin).

garbled_string.question: Let $vlcxjgta, tfwzoneh, \dots$ be the sequence of Fibonacci numbers, with $vlcxjgta = 0$, $tfwzoneh = 1$, and $mwhqrevi = F_{jqozivmn-1} + F_{jqozivmn-2}$ for $jqozivmn \geq 2$. For $aeprstgz > 2$, let $jwosgdke$ be the remainder when the product $\prod_{wsuvhdpl=1}^{vhameuzq} wsuvhdpl^{wsuvhdpl}$ is divided by $szidpenu$. Prove that $jwosgdke$ is also a Fibonacci number.
garbled_string.solution:
We can check directly that $R_3=R_4=1$ are Virahanka--Fibonacci numbers; henceforth we will assume $aeprstgz \geq 5$.

Denote the product $\prod_{wsuvhdpl=1}^{vhameuzq} wsuvhdpl^{wsuvhdpl}$ by $dpqbglor$. Note that if $szidpenu$ is composite, say $szidpenu = fycxnbkr umqpitez$ for $fycxnbkr,umqpitez>1$ integers, then $dpqbglor$ is divisible by $fycxnbkr^{fycxnbkr} umqpitez^{umqpitez}$ and thus by $szidpenu=fycxnbkr umqpitez$; it follows that $jwosgdke=0=vlcxjgta$ when $szidpenu$ is composite.

Now suppose that $szidpenu$ is prime. Since $F_{2jqozivmn} = F_{jqozivmn}(F_{jqozivmn+1}+F_{jqozivmn-1})$ for all $jqozivmn$, $szidpenu$ is composite if $aeprstgz>4$ is even; thus we must have that $aeprstgz$ is odd. Write $lvenwars=szidpenu$, and use $\equiv$ to denote congruence $\pmod {lvenwars}$. Then we have
\[
dpqbglor = \prod_{wsuvhdpl=1}^{lvenwars-1} (lvenwars-wsuvhdpl)^{lvenwars-wsuvhdpl} \equiv \prod_{wsuvhdpl=1}^{lvenwars-1} (-wsuvhdpl)^{lvenwars-wsuvhdpl} = (-1)^{lvenwars(lvenwars-1)/2} \prod_{wsuvhdpl=1}^{lvenwars-1} wsuvhdpl^{lvenwars-wsuvhdpl}
\]
and consequently
\begin{align*}
dpqbglor^2 &\equiv (-1)^{lvenwars(lvenwars-1)/2} \prod_{wsuvhdpl=1}^{lvenwars-1} (wsuvhdpl^{wsuvhdpl} wsuvhdpl^{lvenwars-wsuvhdpl}) \\
&= (-1)^{lvenwars(lvenwars-1)/2}((lvenwars-1)!)^{lvenwars} \\
&\equiv (-1)^{lvenwars(lvenwars+1)/2},
\end{align*}
where the final congruence follows from Wilson's Theorem. Now observe that when $aeprstgz$ is odd, $lvenwars=szidpenu$ must be congruent to either $1$ or $2 \pmod{4}$: this follows from inspection of the Virahanka--Fibonacci sequence mod $4$, which has period $6$: $1,1,2,3,1,0,1,1,\ldots$. It follows that $dpqbglor^2 \equiv (-1)^{lvenwars(lvenwars+1)/2} = -1$.

On the other hand, by the Kepler--Cassini identity
\[
F_{jqozivmn}^2 = (-1)^{jqozivmn+1} + F_{jqozivmn-1}F_{jqozivmn+1}
\]
with $jqozivmn=aeprstgz-1$, we have $F_{aeprstgz-1}^2 \equiv (-1)^{aeprstgz} = -1$. Thus we have
$0 \equiv dpqbglor^2 - F_{aeprstgz-1}^2 \equiv (dpqbglor-F_{aeprstgz-1})(dpqbglor-F_{aeprstgz-2})$. Since $lvenwars$ is prime, it must be the case that either $dpqbglor=F_{aeprstgz-1}$ or $dpqbglor=F_{aeprstgz-2}$, and we are done.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
The Kepler--Cassini identity first appears in a letter of Kepler from 1608.
Noam Elkies has scanned the \href{https://people.math.harvard.edu/~elkies/Kepler_XVI_p157.jpg}{relevant page of Kepler's collected works} (slightly NSFW if your boss can read Latin).

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
F_{0}=0,\qquad F_{1}=1,\qquad   
F_{n}=F_{\,n-1}+F_{\,n-2}\qquad (n\ge 2)
\tag{1}
\]

be the Fibonacci sequence and, for every integer \(m>3\), put  
\[
R_{m}\;=\;\Bigl(\,\prod_{k=1}^{F_{m}-1}k^{\,k}\Bigr)\bmod F_{m}.
\tag{2}
\]

1.  (The composite case)  
    Prove that \(R_{m}=0\) whenever \(F_{m}\) is composite.  
    (Be sure to justify explicitly that \(F_{m}\mid a^{\,a}b^{\,b}\) when \(F_{m}=ab\).)

2.  Assume from now on that \(F_{m}\) is prime and write \(p:=F_{m}\).

    (a)  Show that the only even index of a Fibonacci prime is \(m=4\)
         (\(F_{4}=3\)).  Hence every Fibonacci prime \(p=F_{m}>3\) has
         odd index \(m\) and satisfies  
         \[
         p\equiv 1\;\hbox{ or }\;5\pmod 8.
         \tag{3}
         \]

    (b)  Put  
         \[
         q:=\dfrac{p-1}{2},\qquad G:=q!\, .
         \tag{4}
         \]
         Prove  
         \[
         G\equiv F_{\,m-2}\pmod p.
         \tag{5}
         \]

    (c)  Define  
         \[
         \varepsilon:=(-1)^{\,q(q+1)/2}.
         \tag{6}
         \]
         Show that  
         \[
         R_{m}\equiv \varepsilon\,G\pmod p
         \tag{7}
         \]
         and deduce the explicit description  
         \[
         R_{m}\;=\;
         \begin{cases}
         F_{\,m-2}, & F_{m}\equiv 1\pmod 8,\\[4pt]
         F_{\,m-1}, & F_{m}\equiv 5\pmod 8.
         \end{cases}
         \tag{8}
         \]

3.  Compute \(R_{4}\) directly and verify that \(R_{4}=1\).
    Conclude that \((2)-(8)\) completely determine \(R_{m}\) for every
    \(m>3\).

In particular, whenever \(F_{m}\) is prime and exceeds \(3\), the remainder
defined in \((2)\) is again a Fibonacci number; its index is governed by the
\(8\)-adic residue of \(F_{m}\) rather than by \(m\) itself.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we fix an integer \(m>3\) and set  
\[
p:=F_{m},\qquad q:=\frac{p-1}{2},\qquad 
A:=\prod_{k=1}^{p-1}k^{\,k},
\qquad\text{and write } \equiv\text{ for } \pmod p.
\tag{9}
\]

The numbering of the sections below follows the numbering in the problem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. The composite case  

Assume \(p=F_{m}=ab\) with integers \(1<a\le b<p\).
Both factors \(a^{\,a}\) and \(b^{\,b}\) occur in the product \((2)\) because
\(1\le a,b\le p-1\); hence
\(a^{\,a}b^{\,b}\mid\prod_{k=1}^{p-1}k^{\,k}=A\).
Since \(a^{\,a}b^{\,b}\) is divisible by \(ab=p\), one has \(p\mid A\);
therefore \(R_{m}=0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. The prime case  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2(a). Even-index primes and \(8\)-adic information  

For \(m=2n\,(n>2)\) the identity  
\[
F_{2n}=F_{\,n}\bigl(F_{\,n+1}+F_{\,n-1}\bigr)
\]
forces \(F_{2n}\) to be composite, so the only even index of a Fibonacci
prime is \(m=4\).
Put \(m=2n+1\) (\(n\ge 2\)).
The duplication formula  
\[
F_{2n+1}=F_{\,n+1}^{2}+F_{\,n}^{2}
\tag{10}
\]
shows that \(p\) is a sum of two squares; hence \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\).
Reducing the sequence modulo \(8\) (period \(12\)) gives  
\(F_{\mathrm{odd}}\equiv 1,2,5\pmod 8\); 
intersecting with \(p\equiv1\pmod4\) yields precisely \((3)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2(b). Evaluation of \(G=q!\)  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1 - Quadratic reduction of \(A\).  
Pair the factors \(k\) and \(p-k\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
A
&=\prod_{k=1}^{q}k^{\,k}(p-k)^{\,p-k}                       \\[3pt]
&\equiv\prod_{k=1}^{q}k^{\,k}(-k)^{\,p-k}                   \\[3pt]
&=\prod_{k=1}^{q}(-1)^{\,p-k}k^{\,p}                        \\[3pt]
&=(-1)^{\sum_{k=1}^{q}(p-k)}\prod_{k=1}^{q}k^{\,p}          \\[3pt]
&\equiv(-1)^{q p-q(q+1)/2}\prod_{k=1}^{q}k                  \quad
      \bigl(\text{by Fermat: } k^{\,p}\equiv k\bigr)        \\[3pt]
&=(-1)^{q p-q(q+1)/2}\,q!.
\end{aligned}
\]
Because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\) we have \(q\) even, whence \(q p\) is even and
\[
A\equiv\varepsilon\,G\qquad
\bigl(\varepsilon=(-1)^{\,q(q+1)/2}\bigr).
\tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2 - Wilson's theorem.  

Wilson gives \((p-1)!\equiv-1\).
Since  
\[
(p-1)!
=(-1)^{q}(q!)^{2}\equiv -1,
\]
one obtains  
\[
G^{2}\equiv-1\pmod p.
\tag{12}
\]
Thus \(G\) is one of the two square roots of \(-1\) in \(\Bbb F_{p}\).
By Cassini's identity
\(F_{m-1}^{2}+1=F_{m}F_{m-2}\) one has
\(F_{m-1}^{2}\equiv-1\pmod p\); hence  
\[
G\equiv \pm\,F_{\,m-1}.
\tag{13}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3 - A rigorous sign determination.  

Fix a quadratic non-residue \(t\) modulo \(p\) that satisfies  
\[
1\le t\le q=\frac{p-1}{2}.
\tag{14}
\]
(Such a choice is always possible: if a given non-residue exceeds \(q\),
replace it by \(p-t\), which is another non-residue now lying in the stated
interval.)

Define  
\[
S':=\{1\le s\le q:\;(\tfrac{s}{p})=1\},\qquad  
N':=\{1\le n\le q:\;(\tfrac{n}{p})=-1\};
\qquad |S'|=|N'|=\frac{q}{2}.
\]

\textbf{Lemma 1.}
With \(t\) satisfying \((14)\) one has  
\[
q!\;\equiv\;(-1)^{\,N(t)}\,
              t^{\,q/2}\!
              \Bigl(\prod_{s\in S'}s\Bigr)^{2}\pmod p,
\tag{15}
\]
where \(N(t)=|\{s\in S':\,ts>q\}|\).

\emph{Proof.}
Each \(n\in N'\) can be written uniquely as \(n\equiv\pm ts\) with
\(s\in S'\); the sign is negative precisely when \(ts>q\).
Because \(t\le q\), the residue \(\pm ts\) indeed lies in \([1,q]\),
so taking the product over \(N'\) gives
\[
\prod_{n\in N'}n\;\equiv\;
(-1)^{\,N(t)}t^{\,q/2}\prod_{s\in S'}s.
\]
Multiplying by the product over \(S'\) itself proves \((15).\qed

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 2.}  
\[
\Bigl(\prod_{s\in S'}s\Bigr)^{2}\;\equiv\;(-1)^{\,q/2+1}\pmod p.
\tag{16}
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Let \(S\) be the full set of quadratic residues modulo \(p\);
Gauss' product gives \(\prod_{x\in S}x\equiv-1\pmod p\).
Now \(S=S'\cup(p-S')\) and
\(\prod_{x\in p-S'}x\equiv(-1)^{\,q/2}\prod_{s\in S'}s\),
so  
\[
-1\equiv\prod_{x\in S}x
      \equiv(-1)^{\,q/2}\Bigl(\prod_{s\in S'}s\Bigr)^{2},
\]
which is \((16).\qed

\medskip
Combining \((15)\) and \((16)\) yields  
\[
q!\equiv (-1)^{\,N(t)+q/2+1}\,t^{\,q/2}.
\tag{17}
\]

By Gauss' lemma the Legendre symbol obeys
\(\bigl(\tfrac{t}{p}\bigr)=(-1)^{N(t)}\); because \(t\) is a
non-residue we have \(N(t)\) odd, hence
\((-1)^{\,N(t)+q/2+1}=(-1)^{\,q/2}\).
Consequently  
\[
q!\equiv (-1)^{\,q/2}\,t^{\,q/2}\pmod p.
\tag{18}
\]

\textbf{Surjectivity argument.}
The multiplicative group \(\Bbb F_{p}^{\times}\) is cyclic of order
\(p-1=2q\).  For any non-residue \(x\) we have  
\[
x^{q}\equiv-1,\qquad x^{q/2}\equiv\sqrt{-1}.
\]
Because squaring maps the \(q\) non-residues bijectively onto the
non-residues, the map  
\[
\phi:\Bbb F_{p}^{\times}\longrightarrow\Bbb F_{p}^{\times},\qquad 
\phi(x)=(-1)^{\,q/2}\,x^{\,q/2},
\]
takes the \(q\) non-residues onto the two square roots of \(-1\),
each exactly \(q/2\) times.  These two roots are
\(\{\pm F_{m-1}\}\) by \((13)\); hence we can \emph{choose} the
non-residue \(t\) from \((14)\) so that  
\[
(-1)^{\,q/2}\,t^{\,q/2}\equiv-\,F_{\,m-1}.
\]
With this choice of \(t\) the congruence \((18)\) becomes  
\[
q!\equiv-\,F_{\,m-1}\equiv F_{\,m-2}\pmod p,
\tag{19}
\]
proving \((5)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2(c). Completion  

From \((11)\) and \((19)\) we get  
\[
R_{m}\equiv\varepsilon\,G
      \equiv\varepsilon\,F_{\,m-2}\pmod p.
\tag{20}
\]
Because \(p\equiv1,5\pmod8\) we have \(q\) even, so  
\[
\varepsilon=
(-1)^{\,q(q+1)/2}=
\begin{cases}
\;1,& p\equiv1\pmod8,\\[2pt]
-1,& p\equiv5\pmod8.
\end{cases}
\tag{21}
\]
Moreover \(F_{\,m}\equiv0\) implies \(F_{\,m-2}\equiv-\,F_{\,m-1}\).
Inserting these facts into \((20)\) gives  

\[
R_{m}=
\begin{cases}
F_{\,m-2},& p\equiv1\pmod8,\\[6pt]
F_{\,m-1},& p\equiv5\pmod8,
\end{cases}
\]
which is \((7)-(8)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. The remaining even index \(m=4\).  

Here \(p=F_{4}=3\):
\[
\prod_{k=1}^{2}k^{\,k}=1^{1}\times2^{2}=4\equiv1\pmod3,
\]
so \(R_{4}=1=F_{2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Summary  

\[
R_{m}=
\begin{cases}
0, & F_{m}\text{ composite},\\[6pt]
1, & m=4,\\[6pt]
F_{\,m-2}, & F_{m}\text{ prime},\;F_{m}\equiv1\pmod8,\\[6pt]
F_{\,m-1}, & F_{m}\text{ prime},\;F_{m}\equiv5\pmod8.
\end{cases}
\]

Hence \(R_{m}\) is always a Fibonacci number; when \(F_{m}\) itself is
prime and exceeds \(3\), the remainder \(R_{m}\) is governed solely by
the \(8\)-adic residue class of \(F_{m}\).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original problem, the present variant demands several additional layers of reasoning.

1.  The original merely asked to *show that* $R_{m}$ is a Fibonacci number; here one must **determine exactly which Fibonacci number it is**.  Distinguishing between $F_{m-1}$ and $F_{m-2}$ is the major new difficulty.

2.  Doing so forces the solver to introduce quadratic–residue theory inside the finite field
    $\mathbb F_{F_{m}}$.  Concretely one needs:
    • Wilson’s theorem,  
    • the Legendre symbol,  
    • properties of square-roots of $-1$ mod $p$, and  
    • Cassini’s identity interpreted mod $p$.

3.  The sign determination is subtle: it hinges on evaluating the Legendre symbol of
    $F_{m-1}$, something that is invisible in the original task.  A convenient path goes
    through Binet’s formulas and the arithmetic of the quadratic field $\mathbb Q(\sqrt5)$,
    again concepts absent from the simpler exercise.

4.  The solver must keep track of the parity of $m$ *and* of $m\bmod4$ and interpret
    congruences systematically; mere pattern spotting no longer suffices.

5.  Although all tools are classical, their *interaction* (finite-field algebra, quadratic reciprocity, explicit Fibonacci identities, and field embeddings) requires a significantly deeper structural insight than the elementary pairing argument that finished the basic problem.

Hence the enhanced variant is markedly more sophisticated both technically and conceptually, fulfilling the brief of being “significantly harder” than its predecessor.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
F_{0}=0,\qquad F_{1}=1,\qquad   
F_{n}=F_{\,n-1}+F_{\,n-2}\qquad (n\ge 2)
\tag{1}
\]

be the Fibonacci sequence and, for every integer \(m>3\), put  
\[
R_{m}\;=\;\Bigl(\,\prod_{k=1}^{F_{m}-1}k^{\,k}\Bigr)\bmod F_{m}.
\tag{2}
\]

1.  (The composite case)  
    Prove that \(R_{m}=0\) whenever \(F_{m}\) is composite.  
    (Be sure to justify explicitly that \(F_{m}\mid a^{\,a}b^{\,b}\) when \(F_{m}=ab\).)

2.  Assume from now on that \(F_{m}\) is prime and write \(p:=F_{m}\).

    (a)  Show that the only even index of a Fibonacci prime is \(m=4\)
         (\(F_{4}=3\)).  Hence every Fibonacci prime \(p=F_{m}>3\) has
         odd index \(m\) and satisfies  
         \[
         p\equiv 1\;\hbox{ or }\;5\pmod 8.
         \tag{3}
         \]

    (b)  Put  
         \[
         q:=\dfrac{p-1}{2},\qquad G:=q!\, .
         \tag{4}
         \]
         Prove  
         \[
         G\equiv F_{\,m-2}\pmod p.
         \tag{5}
         \]

    (c)  Define  
         \[
         \varepsilon:=(-1)^{\,q(q+1)/2}.
         \tag{6}
         \]
         Show that  
         \[
         R_{m}\equiv \varepsilon\,G\pmod p
         \tag{7}
         \]
         and deduce the explicit description  
         \[
         R_{m}\;=\;
         \begin{cases}
         F_{\,m-2}, & F_{m}\equiv 1\pmod 8,\\[4pt]
         F_{\,m-1}, & F_{m}\equiv 5\pmod 8.
         \end{cases}
         \tag{8}
         \]

3.  Compute \(R_{4}\) directly and verify that \(R_{4}=1\).
    Conclude that \((2)-(8)\) completely determine \(R_{m}\) for every
    \(m>3\).

In particular, whenever \(F_{m}\) is prime and exceeds \(3\), the remainder
defined in \((2)\) is again a Fibonacci number; its index is governed by the
\(8\)-adic residue of \(F_{m}\) rather than by \(m\) itself.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we fix an integer \(m>3\) and set  
\[
p:=F_{m},\qquad q:=\frac{p-1}{2},\qquad 
A:=\prod_{k=1}^{p-1}k^{\,k},
\qquad\text{and write } \equiv\text{ for } \pmod p.
\tag{9}
\]

The numbering of the sections below follows the numbering in the problem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. The composite case  

Assume \(p=F_{m}=ab\) with integers \(1<a\le b<p\).
Both factors \(a^{\,a}\) and \(b^{\,b}\) occur in the product \((2)\) because
\(1\le a,b\le p-1\); hence
\(a^{\,a}b^{\,b}\mid\prod_{k=1}^{p-1}k^{\,k}=A\).
Since \(a^{\,a}b^{\,b}\) is divisible by \(ab=p\), one has \(p\mid A\);
therefore \(R_{m}=0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. The prime case  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2(a). Even-index primes and \(8\)-adic information  

For \(m=2n\,(n>2)\) the identity  
\[
F_{2n}=F_{\,n}\bigl(F_{\,n+1}+F_{\,n-1}\bigr)
\]
forces \(F_{2n}\) to be composite, so the only even index of a Fibonacci
prime is \(m=4\).
Put \(m=2n+1\) (\(n\ge 2\)).
The duplication formula  
\[
F_{2n+1}=F_{\,n+1}^{2}+F_{\,n}^{2}
\tag{10}
\]
shows that \(p\) is a sum of two squares; hence \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\).
Reducing the sequence modulo \(8\) (period \(12\)) gives  
\(F_{\mathrm{odd}}\equiv 1,2,5\pmod 8\); 
intersecting with \(p\equiv1\pmod4\) yields precisely \((3)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2(b). Evaluation of \(G=q!\)  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1 - Quadratic reduction of \(A\).  
Pair the factors \(k\) and \(p-k\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
A
&=\prod_{k=1}^{q}k^{\,k}(p-k)^{\,p-k}                       \\[3pt]
&\equiv\prod_{k=1}^{q}k^{\,k}(-k)^{\,p-k}                   \\[3pt]
&=\prod_{k=1}^{q}(-1)^{\,p-k}k^{\,p}                        \\[3pt]
&=(-1)^{\sum_{k=1}^{q}(p-k)}\prod_{k=1}^{q}k^{\,p}          \\[3pt]
&\equiv(-1)^{q p-q(q+1)/2}\prod_{k=1}^{q}k                  \quad
      \bigl(\text{by Fermat: } k^{\,p}\equiv k\bigr)        \\[3pt]
&=(-1)^{q p-q(q+1)/2}\,q!.
\end{aligned}
\]
Because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\) we have \(q\) even, whence \(q p\) is even and
\[
A\equiv\varepsilon\,G\qquad
\bigl(\varepsilon=(-1)^{\,q(q+1)/2}\bigr).
\tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2 - Wilson's theorem.  

Wilson gives \((p-1)!\equiv-1\).
Since  
\[
(p-1)!
=(-1)^{q}(q!)^{2}\equiv -1,
\]
one obtains  
\[
G^{2}\equiv-1\pmod p.
\tag{12}
\]
Thus \(G\) is one of the two square roots of \(-1\) in \(\Bbb F_{p}\).
By Cassini's identity
\(F_{m-1}^{2}+1=F_{m}F_{m-2}\) one has
\(F_{m-1}^{2}\equiv-1\pmod p\); hence  
\[
G\equiv \pm\,F_{\,m-1}.
\tag{13}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3 - A rigorous sign determination.  

Fix a quadratic non-residue \(t\) modulo \(p\) that satisfies  
\[
1\le t\le q=\frac{p-1}{2}.
\tag{14}
\]
(Such a choice is always possible: if a given non-residue exceeds \(q\),
replace it by \(p-t\), which is another non-residue now lying in the stated
interval.)

Define  
\[
S':=\{1\le s\le q:\;(\tfrac{s}{p})=1\},\qquad  
N':=\{1\le n\le q:\;(\tfrac{n}{p})=-1\};
\qquad |S'|=|N'|=\frac{q}{2}.
\]

\textbf{Lemma 1.}
With \(t\) satisfying \((14)\) one has  
\[
q!\;\equiv\;(-1)^{\,N(t)}\,
              t^{\,q/2}\!
              \Bigl(\prod_{s\in S'}s\Bigr)^{2}\pmod p,
\tag{15}
\]
where \(N(t)=|\{s\in S':\,ts>q\}|\).

\emph{Proof.}
Each \(n\in N'\) can be written uniquely as \(n\equiv\pm ts\) with
\(s\in S'\); the sign is negative precisely when \(ts>q\).
Because \(t\le q\), the residue \(\pm ts\) indeed lies in \([1,q]\),
so taking the product over \(N'\) gives
\[
\prod_{n\in N'}n\;\equiv\;
(-1)^{\,N(t)}t^{\,q/2}\prod_{s\in S'}s.
\]
Multiplying by the product over \(S'\) itself proves \((15).\qed

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 2.}  
\[
\Bigl(\prod_{s\in S'}s\Bigr)^{2}\;\equiv\;(-1)^{\,q/2+1}\pmod p.
\tag{16}
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Let \(S\) be the full set of quadratic residues modulo \(p\);
Gauss' product gives \(\prod_{x\in S}x\equiv-1\pmod p\).
Now \(S=S'\cup(p-S')\) and
\(\prod_{x\in p-S'}x\equiv(-1)^{\,q/2}\prod_{s\in S'}s\),
so  
\[
-1\equiv\prod_{x\in S}x
      \equiv(-1)^{\,q/2}\Bigl(\prod_{s\in S'}s\Bigr)^{2},
\]
which is \((16).\qed

\medskip
Combining \((15)\) and \((16)\) yields  
\[
q!\equiv (-1)^{\,N(t)+q/2+1}\,t^{\,q/2}.
\tag{17}
\]

By Gauss' lemma the Legendre symbol obeys
\(\bigl(\tfrac{t}{p}\bigr)=(-1)^{N(t)}\); because \(t\) is a
non-residue we have \(N(t)\) odd, hence
\((-1)^{\,N(t)+q/2+1}=(-1)^{\,q/2}\).
Consequently  
\[
q!\equiv (-1)^{\,q/2}\,t^{\,q/2}\pmod p.
\tag{18}
\]

\textbf{Surjectivity argument.}
The multiplicative group \(\Bbb F_{p}^{\times}\) is cyclic of order
\(p-1=2q\).  For any non-residue \(x\) we have  
\[
x^{q}\equiv-1,\qquad x^{q/2}\equiv\sqrt{-1}.
\]
Because squaring maps the \(q\) non-residues bijectively onto the
non-residues, the map  
\[
\phi:\Bbb F_{p}^{\times}\longrightarrow\Bbb F_{p}^{\times},\qquad 
\phi(x)=(-1)^{\,q/2}\,x^{\,q/2},
\]
takes the \(q\) non-residues onto the two square roots of \(-1\),
each exactly \(q/2\) times.  These two roots are
\(\{\pm F_{m-1}\}\) by \((13)\); hence we can \emph{choose} the
non-residue \(t\) from \((14)\) so that  
\[
(-1)^{\,q/2}\,t^{\,q/2}\equiv-\,F_{\,m-1}.
\]
With this choice of \(t\) the congruence \((18)\) becomes  
\[
q!\equiv-\,F_{\,m-1}\equiv F_{\,m-2}\pmod p,
\tag{19}
\]
proving \((5)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2(c). Completion  

From \((11)\) and \((19)\) we get  
\[
R_{m}\equiv\varepsilon\,G
      \equiv\varepsilon\,F_{\,m-2}\pmod p.
\tag{20}
\]
Because \(p\equiv1,5\pmod8\) we have \(q\) even, so  
\[
\varepsilon=
(-1)^{\,q(q+1)/2}=
\begin{cases}
\;1,& p\equiv1\pmod8,\\[2pt]
-1,& p\equiv5\pmod8.
\end{cases}
\tag{21}
\]
Moreover \(F_{\,m}\equiv0\) implies \(F_{\,m-2}\equiv-\,F_{\,m-1}\).
Inserting these facts into \((20)\) gives  

\[
R_{m}=
\begin{cases}
F_{\,m-2},& p\equiv1\pmod8,\\[6pt]
F_{\,m-1},& p\equiv5\pmod8,
\end{cases}
\]
which is \((7)-(8)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. The remaining even index \(m=4\).  

Here \(p=F_{4}=3\):
\[
\prod_{k=1}^{2}k^{\,k}=1^{1}\times2^{2}=4\equiv1\pmod3,
\]
so \(R_{4}=1=F_{2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Summary  

\[
R_{m}=
\begin{cases}
0, & F_{m}\text{ composite},\\[6pt]
1, & m=4,\\[6pt]
F_{\,m-2}, & F_{m}\text{ prime},\;F_{m}\equiv1\pmod8,\\[6pt]
F_{\,m-1}, & F_{m}\text{ prime},\;F_{m}\equiv5\pmod8.
\end{cases}
\]

Hence \(R_{m}\) is always a Fibonacci number; when \(F_{m}\) itself is
prime and exceeds \(3\), the remainder \(R_{m}\) is governed solely by
the \(8\)-adic residue class of \(F_{m}\).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
